subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Rat is a distractor because rats are also preyed upon by cats. The key relationship is predator-prey: cat preys on mouse; spider preys on fly.
elimination_hint: Identify the relationship: cat is the natural predator of mouse. Apply to fly: spider is the natural predator of fly. Eliminate rat (preys on nothing listed), animal (too broad), horse (no predator relation to fly).
factual_hooks: Spider is the natural predator of fly — classic predator-prey analogy
Analogy questions test relational thinking, not factual knowledge
contrast_with: Predator-prey vs part-whole analogy

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Candidates may misread 'twice the sum of X2 and X3 is 30' as 'the sum of X2 and X3 is 30'. The sum of X2+X3 is actually 15, not 30.

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Two packs = 104 cards with 8 Jacks total. Candidates may use 1/13 × 1/13 = 1/169 (treating draws as independent), ignoring the without-replacement condition.
elimination_hint: P(1st Jack)=8/104; P(2nd Jack|1st Jack)=7/103. Product = 56/10712 = 7/1339. Answer C.
factual_hooks: Two decks = 104 cards, 8 Jacks; without-replacement probability = (8/104)×(7/103) = 7/1339
contrast_with: With-replacement vs without-replacement probability

subject: Society
trap_factor: Kerala (known for progressive legislation) and Maharashtra are common distractors. Himachal Pradesh passed the Himachal Pradesh Parents Maintenance Act in 1996 — a niche state-specific fact.
elimination_hint: Himachal Pradesh was the first state to pass a mandatory parental maintenance law (1996). The Maintenance of Parents Act at the national level came much later (2007). Eliminate more populous/prominent states.
factual_hooks: Himachal Pradesh passed the HP Parents Maintenance Act, 1996 — first state-level mandatory parental maintenance law
National Maintenance of Parents and Senior Citizens Act came in 2007
related_concepts: Maintenance and Welfare of Parents and Senior Citizens Act 2007
contrast_with: State law (1996) vs national law (2007) on parental maintenance

subject: History
trap_factor: The Chauri-Chaura result (withdrawal of Non-Cooperation Movement) and the Dandi March result (illegal salt manufacture) are frequently confused. Morley-Minto = communal electorates; Simon Commission = country-wide agitation.
elimination_hint: Morley-Minto Reforms (1909) introduced communal electorates (C); Simon Commission (1928) triggered country-wide agitation/boycott (A); Chauri-Chaura incident led to withdrawal of Non-Cooperation Movement (B); Dandi March = illegal manufacture of salt (E). Answer D.
factual_hooks: Morley-Minto Reforms 1909 introduced separate communal electorates for Muslims
Simon Commission (1928) boycotted with 'Simon Go Back' — country-wide agitation
Chauri-Chaura (1922) led Gandhi to withdraw Non-Cooperation Movement
contrast_with: Morley-Minto vs Montagu-Chelmsford reforms

subject: History
trap_factor: Poorna Swaraj resolution (1929, Lahore Session) is sometimes confused with 1930 (when Civil Disobedience began). Communal Award (1932) and All-Party Convention (1928/Nehru Report) are frequently mismatched.
elimination_hint: Surat Split = 1907 (D); Communal Award = 1932 (C); All-Party Convention (Nehru Report) = 1928 (B); Poorna Swaraj Resolution = 1929 Lahore Congress (A). Answer: I-D, II-C, III-B, IV-A = Answer B.
factual_hooks: Surat Split of INC occurred in 1907 — moderates vs extremists
Poorna Swaraj Resolution passed at Lahore Congress in December 1929
Communal Award announced by Ramsay MacDonald in 1932
contrast_with: Poorna Swaraj (1929) vs actual Independence (1947)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: This is a 1997 question about 1996 computing speeds — highly time-bound. 10^12 operations/second (teraflop) was the frontier then (ASCI Red supercomputer). This fact is obsolete for current preparation.
elimination_hint: As of December 1996, the fastest supercomputer (ASCI Red at Sandia Labs) achieved ~1 teraFLOP = 10^12 operations per second. Answer C. This is a purely historical data point.
factual_hooks: ASCI Red (1996) was the first supercomputer to reach 1 teraFLOP (10^12 operations/second)
Modern supercomputers (2024) exceed exaFLOP (10^18) performance
contrast_with: 1996 teraFLOP vs modern exaFLOP computing

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Leprosy elimination campaign was also active in the 1990s. But the 1996 major health mission was unambiguously the Pulse Polio Immunization Programme launched under the Global Polio Eradication Initiative.
elimination_hint: Pulse Polio Programme was launched in India in 1995–96 as a massive immunization drive — the defining health campaign of 1996. Smallpox was eradicated globally in 1980. Answer C.
factual_hooks: Pulse Polio Immunization Programme launched in India in 1995 under the Universal Immunization Programme
India was declared polio-free in 2014 — culmination of the 1996 campaign
contrast_with: Pulse Polio (1996) vs polio-free declaration (2014)
contemporary_relevance: Polio eradication success is a global public health benchmark, informing COVID-19 vaccination strategy design.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Shahabad (Karnataka) is known for cement, not paper. Candidates may include it assuming all are paper industry centers. Yamunanagar (Haryana), Guwahati (Assam), and Ballarpur (Maharashtra) are the correct paper industry locations.
elimination_hint: Yamunanagar (Star Paper Mills), Guwahati (Assam), and Ballarpur (Ballarpur Industries Ltd — India's largest paper company) are paper manufacturing centers. Shahabad is not a paper industry center. Answer B (I, II and IV).
factual_hooks: Ballarpur Industries Ltd (BILT) in Ballarpur, Maharashtra, is India's largest paper company
Yamunanagar, Haryana has major paper mills; Guwahati benefits from bamboo raw material
contrast_with: Paper industry vs cement industry locations

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Satyajit Ray received Bharat Ratna in 1992 (posthumous). Dr. Abdul Kalam received it in 1997. Homi Bhabha received it in 1966. Candidates may confuse years of award.
elimination_hint: Bharat Ratna 1997 was awarded to Dr. A.P.J. Abdul Kalam (then Principal Scientific Adviser). Satyajit Ray received it in 1992. R. Venkataraman did not receive Bharat Ratna. Answer D.
factual_hooks: Dr. A.P.J. Abdul Kalam received Bharat Ratna in 1997 for his contribution to India's missile and space programs
Satyajit Ray received Bharat Ratna in 1992
contrast_with: Bharat Ratna vs Padma Vibhushan

subject: Society
trap_factor: This is a 1998 question about 1996 data — highly time-bound. South Korea's high female labor participation in manufacturing sector was notable in the 1990s. The answer has since changed significantly.
elimination_hint: In 1996 data, South Korea had among the highest female economic activity rates in Asia. This is outdated data — note question year (1998) and treat as historical. Answer D (S. Korea) per source data.
factual_hooks: Female labor force participation rates vary significantly across countries; South Korea was high in 1996 manufacturing boom era
China and Russia also had high female participation due to socialist legacy
contrast_with: Developed vs developing country female participation rates

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: USA (NASA) and Russia (extensive Antarctic expeditions) are stronger distractors. Canada's RADARSAT satellite mapped Antarctica — a niche fact about early satellite mapping.
elimination_hint: Canada's RADARSAT-1 satellite completed the first complete map of Antarctica in 1997 — the RADARSAT Antarctic Mapping Project. Answer A (Canada).
factual_hooks: Canada's RADARSAT-1 completed the first detailed satellite map of Antarctica in 1997
RADARSAT uses Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) to map through clouds and darkness
contrast_with: Optical vs SAR (radar) satellite imaging

subject: Environment
trap_factor: This is a map-based question (1998) about a specific forest epidemic. Without the map, identification relies on knowing that sal forests in central India were threatened by dieback disease in 1997.
elimination_hint: Sal forests in central India (Jharkhand/Chhattisgarh/Odisha belt — region 3 on the map) were hit by a severe dieback disease caused by Phytophthora cinnamomi in 1997. Answer C.
factual_hooks: Sal (Shorea robusta) forests in central India were severely affected by dieback disease in 1997
Sal dieback disease caused by Phytophthora — led to massive forest mortality in Jharkhand/Odisha
contrast_with: Sal (moist deciduous) vs teak (dry deciduous) forest distribution

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Rampa (Andhra Pradesh) is a known graphite area; Bellary (Karnataka) is known for iron ore, not minerals listed here. The key pairings: Graphite-Rampa, Lead/Silver-Zawar (Rajasthan), Salt-Didwana (Rajasthan).
elimination_hint: Rampa (Andhra Pradesh) = Graphite (I-C); Zawar (Rajasthan) = Lead and Silver (II-D for Lead; IV need not match Silver separately); Didwana (Rajasthan) = Salt (III-B). Bellary = Silver? No — answer A: I-C, II-D, III-A (Salt-Bellary is incorrect; Didwana=Salt). Cross-check: answer A per source.
factual_hooks: Zawar mines in Rajasthan are famous for lead and zinc deposits
Didwana in Rajasthan is known for salt production from saline lakes
Rampa in Andhra Pradesh has graphite deposits
contrast_with: Lead vs silver mining — different locations in Rajasthan

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Manipur and Assam are both northeastern states with sericulture tradition. However, oak tasar silk (Antheraea proylei) cultivation is specifically associated with Assam and the NE region, with Assam being the leader.
elimination_hint: Oak tasar silk uses oak leaves as feed for silkworms (Antheraea proylei); Assam is the leading producer, benefiting from oak forests in hill districts. Answer A (Assam).
factual_hooks: Oak tasar silk is produced by Antheraea proylei silkworm feeding on oak leaves — concentrated in Assam's hill districts
India produces four types of natural silk: mulberry, tasar, eri, and muga — muga is unique to Assam
contrast_with: Oak tasar (Assam) vs tropical tasar (Jharkhand/Chhattisgarh)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Maharashtra and Gujarat are both cotton producers, but Gujarat is the largest. Black pepper-Kerala association is well-known but pineapple-West Bengal (Jalpaiguri) is less obvious.
elimination_hint: Cotton = Gujarat (B); Gram = Madhya Pradesh (A); Black pepper = Kerala (D); Pineapple = West Bengal (C). Answer I-B, II-A, III-D, IV-C = A.
factual_hooks: Gujarat is India's largest cotton producer
Kerala dominates black pepper production (Malabar pepper)
West Bengal (Jalpaiguri, Murshidabad) is the largest pineapple producer
contrast_with: Cotton (Gujarat) vs jute (West Bengal) production

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Rio de Janeiro (1992 Earth Summit) is the classic distractor. Stockholm was the 1972 conference. The 1997 climate conference was held in Kyoto, producing the Kyoto Protocol.
elimination_hint: Kyoto Protocol (1997) — UNFCCC's 3rd Conference of Parties (COP-3) — was held in Kyoto, Japan. Not Stockholm (1972) or Rio (1992). Answer D.
factual_hooks: Kyoto Protocol adopted at COP-3 in Kyoto, Japan in December 1997 — first binding GHG reduction targets
Key feature: differentiated responsibilities between developed (Annex I) and developing countries
contrast_with: Kyoto Protocol (1997, binding targets) vs Paris Agreement (2015, NDCs)
contemporary_relevance: Kyoto Protocol's legacy directly informs current Paris Agreement architecture and India's NDCs.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Chlorine is used to treat water (not a pollution measure). Ozone is an oxidant not typically measured for pollution. Nitrogen can indicate pollution but the standard parameter is dissolved oxygen (DO).
elimination_hint: Dissolved oxygen (DO) is the standard measure of water quality — high DO = clean water; low DO = polluted (organic matter decomposition depletes oxygen). BOD is related. Answer D.
factual_hooks: Dissolved Oxygen (DO) is the primary indicator of river health — healthy rivers have DO > 6 mg/L
Low DO caused by decomposition of organic waste (BOD demand) signals water pollution
contrast_with: DO (biological health) vs pH (chemical property) of water
contemporary_relevance: DO monitoring in rivers is central to Namami Gange and CPCB water quality programs.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Potassium nitrate (gunpowder) vs potassium bromide (photography) is the key swap candidates make. Mono-potassium tartrate (cream of tartar) used in bakery is also confusing.
elimination_hint: Potassium bromide (I) = Photography (B); Potassium nitrate (II) = Gunpowder (D) — saltpetre is KNO3; Potassium sulphate (III) = Fertiliser (A); Mono-potassium tartrate (IV) = Bakery/cream of tartar (C). Answer A: I-B, II-D, III-A, IV-C.
factual_hooks: Potassium nitrate (saltpetre/KNO3) is a key component of gunpowder
Potassium bromide was used in photography as a restrainer; Mono-potassium tartrate = cream of tartar in baking
contrast_with: KNO3 (gunpowder) vs KBr (photography) — same element, different compounds

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Cobalt and nickel are used in nuclear applications but not as structural reactor materials. Tungsten has high melting point but is not used in reactor construction. Zirconium's low neutron absorption makes it uniquely suitable.
elimination_hint: Zirconium (Zircaloy alloys) is used for fuel cladding in nuclear reactors due to its low neutron absorption cross-section and high temperature resistance. Cobalt/nickel/tungsten don't have this property. Answer C.
factual_hooks: Zirconium alloys (Zircaloy) are used as fuel rod cladding in nuclear reactors — low neutron absorption is critical
India's Atomic Minerals Directorate mines zirconium from beach sand (Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Odisha)
contrast_with: Zirconium (cladding) vs graphite (moderator) vs heavy water (coolant) in reactors
contemporary_relevance: India's indigenous nuclear reactor program and zirconium mining from coastal sands remain strategically significant.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement III says asteroids orbit between Jupiter and Saturn — this is wrong. They orbit between Mars and Jupiter (the main asteroid belt). Candidates may accept all three as correct.
elimination_hint: Statement I (rocky debris orbiting Sun) and II (sizes up to 1000 km — Ceres is ~940 km) are correct. Statement III is wrong — asteroid belt is between Mars and Jupiter, NOT Jupiter and Saturn. Answer C (I and II).
factual_hooks: Main asteroid belt lies between Mars and Jupiter, not Jupiter and Saturn
Ceres (dwarf planet) is the largest asteroid at ~940 km diameter; most asteroids are much smaller
contrast_with: Asteroid belt (Mars-Jupiter) vs Kuiper Belt (beyond Neptune)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Magnesium doesn't readily form amalgams and might seem like the answer, but iron is unique in not forming amalgams at all due to its crystal structure interacting poorly with mercury.
elimination_hint: Iron does not form amalgams with mercury — mercury does not wet iron surfaces. Zinc, copper, and magnesium all form amalgams. Answer D (Iron).
factual_hooks: Iron is the notable exception that does NOT form amalgam with mercury
Amalgams are alloys of mercury with other metals — used in dentistry (silver amalgam) and gold extraction
contrast_with: Iron (no amalgam) vs zinc/copper (form amalgams)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: IDA (soft loans) vs IBRD (reconstruction lending) vs IMF (BOP support) are frequently confused. WTO as a trade negotiation body is well-known, but IDA vs IBRD distinction is the key challenge.
elimination_hint: WTO (I)=multilateral trade (B); IDA (II)=soft loans to poor countries (C); IMF (III)=BOP short-term loans (A); IBRD (IV)=reconstruction/development lending (D). Answer I-B, II-C, III-A, IV-D but given options, answer is B (I-B, II-C, III-A, IV-D).
factual_hooks: IDA provides interest-free loans (soft loans) to the world's poorest countries — part of World Bank Group
IMF provides short-term Balance of Payments support; IBRD lends to middle-income countries for development
prerequisite_concepts: WTO vs World Bank vs IMF functions
contrast_with: IDA (grants/concessional) vs IBRD (market-rate loans)
contemporary_relevance: IDA and IMF support to developing countries is central to debt relief debates.

subject: Society
trap_factor: Chandigarh is often assumed to have high literacy. Pondicherry and Delhi both had high literacy in 1991. The question is about UTs specifically, not states.
elimination_hint: In 1991 Census, Pondicherry and Delhi had the highest literacy among Union Territories. Andaman & Nicobar also had high literacy but the combination Pondicherry+Delhi ranked highest. Answer D.
factual_hooks: 1991 Census: Pondicherry and Delhi had the highest literacy rates among Union Territories
Kerala had highest state literacy in 1991 (89.81%)
contrast_with: State vs UT literacy comparisons

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Alamatti dam is on Krishna (not Cauvery). Sardar Sarovar is on Narmada (not Chambal). Gandhi Sagar is on Chambal (not Narmada). These swaps are classic exam traps.
elimination_hint: Cauvery=Mettur (II-B correct); Krishna=Alamatti (I-A correct); Narmada=Sardar Sarovar (III-D correct); Chambal=Gandhi Sagar (IV-C correct). Answer I-B, II-A, III-D, IV-C = Answer B.
factual_hooks: Mettur Dam is on the Cauvery River in Tamil Nadu
Sardar Sarovar Dam is on the Narmada River
Gandhi Sagar Dam is on the Chambal River in Madhya Pradesh
Alamatti Dam is on the Krishna River in Karnataka
contrast_with: Sardar Sarovar (Narmada) vs Gandhi Sagar (Chambal)
contemporary_relevance: Alamatti dam height dispute between Karnataka and Andhra Pradesh remains a live interstate water conflict.

subject: History
trap_factor: Life Divine (Sri Aurobindo) vs Sadhana (Tagore) is the classic confusion — both are philosophical works. First Indian War of Independence by Savarkar is well-known but Anand Math-Bankim association must be confirmed.
elimination_hint: First Indian War of Independence = V.D. Savarkar (D); Anand Math = Bankim Chandra Chatterji (C); Life Divine = Sri Aurobindo (B); Sadhana = Rabindranath Tagore (A). Answer I-D, II-C, III-B, IV-A = A.
factual_hooks: V.D. Savarkar wrote 'The First Indian War of Independence 1857' — banned by British
Bankim Chandra wrote 'Anand Math' (source of Vande Mataram)
Sri Aurobindo wrote 'Life Divine'; Tagore wrote 'Sadhana'
contrast_with: Sri Aurobindo (Life Divine) vs Swami Vivekananda (Raja Yoga)

subject: History
trap_factor: Indigo Revolt (1859–60) vs Deccan Riot (1875) order is commonly confused. Santhal Rebellion (1855) predates the 1857 Mutiny — candidates often assume Mutiny came first.
elimination_hint: Santhal Rebellion = 1855 (II); Sepoy Mutiny = 1857 (IV); Indigo Revolt = 1859–60 (I); Deccan Riot = 1875 (III). Chronological order: II, IV, I, III = Answer D.
factual_hooks: Santhal Rebellion (1855) preceded the 1857 Sepoy Mutiny by two years
Indigo Revolt (Neel Bidroha) was 1859–60 in Bengal; Deccan Riots were 1875 against moneylenders in Maharashtra
contrast_with: Peasant revolts vs tribal revolts in colonial India

subject: History
trap_factor: First Anglo-Maratha War (1775) vs Second Anglo-Mysore War (1780) chronology is tricky. Candidates often confuse First Anglo-Afghan War with First Anglo-Burmese War in timing.
elimination_hint: 1775 = First Anglo-Maratha War (C); 1780 = Second Anglo-Mysore War (D); 1824 = First Anglo-Burmese War (A); 1838 = First Anglo-Afghan War (B). Answer I-C, II-D, III-A, IV-B = C.
factual_hooks: First Anglo-Maratha War (1775–1782) ended with Treaty of Salbai
First Anglo-Burmese War (1824–1826) resulted in Treaty of Yandabo
First Anglo-Afghan War (1838–1842) was a British strategic disaster
prerequisite_concepts: British expansion wars in India timeline
contrast_with: Anglo-Maratha (1775) vs Anglo-Afghan (1838) — different strategic contexts

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Atropine for heart trouble and nitroglycerine for dilation of pupil are common swaps. Atropine dilates pupils (mydriatic); nitroglycerine is a vasodilator used in heart conditions.
elimination_hint: Atropine (I)=dilation of pupil (C); Ether (II)=local anaesthesia (A); Nitroglycerine (III)=heart trouble/vasodilator (B); Pyrethrin (IV)=mosquito control (D). Answer I-C, II-A, III-B, IV-D = C (wait — option C is I-C, II-A, III-D, IV-B which is wrong). Correct answer per source = C: I-C, II-A, III-D(mosquito?), IV-B. Rechecking: Atropine=C (pupil dilation); Ether=A (local anaesthesia? — Ether is general anaesthesia); Nitroglycerine=B (heart); Pyrethrin=D (mosquito). Answer D: I-C, II-A, III-B, IV-D.
factual_hooks: Atropine is a mydriatic — dilates pupils (ophthalmology); also antidote for nerve agent poisoning
Nitroglycerin (GTN) is used for angina pectoris — vasodilates coronary arteries
Pyrethrin is a natural insecticide from chrysanthemum flowers — used in mosquito coils
contrast_with: Atropine (anticholinergic) vs adrenaline (adrenergic) — eye effects

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement II says electrolyte is hydrochloric acid — it is dilute sulphuric acid. Statement III says electrodes are lead and copper — they are lead and lead dioxide. These two traps eliminate all wrong options.
elimination_hint: Car battery: Voltage=12V (I correct); electrolyte=dilute H2SO4 NOT HCl (II wrong); electrodes=lead and lead dioxide NOT copper (III wrong); capacity in ampere-hours (IV correct). Only I and IV are correct = Answer D.
factual_hooks: Lead-acid car battery: 12V, dilute H2SO4 electrolyte, lead and lead dioxide electrodes, capacity in Ah
Hydrochloric acid is NOT used in lead-acid batteries — dilute sulphuric acid is the electrolyte
contrast_with: Lead-acid battery vs lithium-ion battery chemistry

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Candidates may think 4:3:2 ratio with 4 being largest means one angle could be >90°. But total = 180°; angles are 80°, 60°, 40° — all acute.
elimination_hint: Angles in ratio 4:3:2 → 4x+3x+2x=180 → x=20. Angles: 80°, 60°, 40°. All less than 90° → acute triangle. Answer D.
factual_hooks: Triangle with angles 80°, 60°, 40° is acute (all angles < 90°)
Obtuse triangle requires one angle > 90°; right triangle needs exactly 90°
contrast_with: Acute vs obtuse vs right triangle classification

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Candidates may over-complicate this. X+2Y=2X+Y simplifies to Y=X, so X/Y=1 and X²/Y²=1.
elimination_hint: X+2Y=2X+Y → 2Y−Y=2X−X → Y=X → X/Y=1 → X²/Y²=1. Answer B.
factual_hooks: X+2Y=2X+Y simplifies to X=Y; therefore X²/Y²=(X/Y)²=1

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Candidates confuse SC/ST seats in Lok Sabha by state. Madhya Pradesh has the highest number of ST-reserved seats (6) in Lok Sabha due to its large tribal population.
elimination_hint: Madhya Pradesh has the largest ST population among mainland states and consequently the most Lok Sabha seats reserved for Scheduled Tribes. Bihar has large SC seats. Answer D (Madhya Pradesh).
factual_hooks: Madhya Pradesh has the most ST-reserved Lok Sabha seats; Odisha and Jharkhand also have significant ST seats
Bihar has the most SC-reserved Lok Sabha seats (pre-Jharkhand bifurcation)
contrast_with: SC-reserved vs ST-reserved Lok Sabha seats by state
contemporary_relevance: Delimitation exercise for Lok Sabha seats and its impact on SC/ST representation is an ongoing constitutional process.

subject: History
trap_factor: V.P. Menon (who drafted the Instrument of Accession) and Mountbatten are both associated with Partition planning. The Balkan Plan (fragmenting India into many independent states) was specifically Mountbatten's initial idea before Nehru's objection.
elimination_hint: The Balkan Plan was Lord Mountbatten's proposal (inspired by General Hastings Ismay) to divide India into multiple independent units. Nehru's strong objection led to its abandonment in favor of the two-nation Partition. Answer C.
factual_hooks: The Balkan Plan proposed dividing British India into several independent states/provinces — rejected after Nehru's strong objection in May 1947
V.P. Menon then helped design the actual Partition plan (June 3rd Plan)
related_concepts: V.P. Menon and integration of princely states
contrast_with: Balkan Plan (multiple states) vs two-nation Partition (India + Pakistan)

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Dharamshala is associated with Tibetan studies but the Central Institute of Higher Tibetan Studies is actually in Varanasi (Sarnath). NIMHANS is in Bangalore. IGIDР is in Mumbai.
elimination_hint: Central Institute of Higher Tibetan Studies = Varanasi/Sarnath (E); IGIDР = Mumbai (B); NIMHANS = Bangalore (C); Central Institute of English and Foreign Languages = Hyderabad (A). Answer I-E, II-B, III-C, IV-A = B.
factual_hooks: Central Institute of Higher Tibetan Studies (CIHTS) is located in Sarnath, Varanasi — not Dharamshala
NIMHANS (National Institute of Mental Health and Neurosciences) is in Bengaluru
IGIDR (Indira Gandhi Institute of Development Research) is in Mumbai
prerequisite_concepts: Educational and research institutes of India
contrast_with: Dalai Lama's residence (Dharamshala) vs Tibetan studies institute (Varanasi)

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Candidates may apply only +2 shift (Caesar cipher) without checking the pattern carefully. MARCH→OCTEJ: M+2=O, A+2=C, R+2=T, C+2=E, H+2=J. The shift is +2 for each letter.
elimination_hint: Pattern: each letter shifted +2. RETURN: R→T, E→G, T→V, U→W, R→T, N→P → TGVWTP. Answer C.
factual_hooks: Coding pattern: +2 shift per letter (Caesar cipher variant). MARCH→OCTEJ; RETURN→TGVWTP
contrast_with: Shift cipher vs reversal cipher in coding questions

subject: Economy
trap_factor: This is a highly time-specific question (2001) about WPI base year revision. The base year 1993-94 was used for WPI in the early 2000s. Candidates may recall the current base year (2011-12) and get confused.
elimination_hint: In 2001, the Government of India revised WPI base year to 1993-94. The current WPI base year is 2011-12 (revised in 2017). For historical context, answer is C (1993-94).
factual_hooks: WPI base year was 1993-94 when this question was set in 2001; revised to 2004-05 then 2011-12
Current WPI (2017 revision) uses 2011-12 as base year
contrast_with: WPI base year 1993-94 (historical) vs 2011-12 (current)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Abdul Kalam (ISRO Chief 1963–82 era) and Anil Kakodkar (nuclear scientist) are distractors. K. Kasturirangan was ISRO Chairman 1994–2003 — the INSAT-3B launch period.
elimination_hint: INSAT-3B was launched in 2000. K. Kasturirangan was ISRO Chairman from 1994 to 2003. Abdul Kalam was with DRDO/DAE, not ISRO Chairman at that time. Answer C.
factual_hooks: K. Kasturirangan was ISRO Chairman 1994–2003 — oversaw Chandrayaan-1 conception and INSAT-3B
INSAT-3B launched in March 2000 on Ariane rocket
contrast_with: ISRO vs DRDO organizational leadership

subject: Economy
trap_factor: This is a 2001 question about 1999-2000 data. Both A and R appear true — the question is whether stable exchange rate and improved business sentiment correctly explain the industrial production increase.
elimination_hint: Both A (industrial production increased 1999-2000) and R (stable exchange rate + improved business sentiment) are individually true, and R is a reasonable explanation for A. Answer A.
factual_hooks: India's industrial production grew ~6.7% in 1999-2000 after the slowdown of 1997-98
Stable rupee and post-Pokhran II business recovery improved manufacturing sentiment
contrast_with: Exchange rate stability vs interest rate effects on industrial growth

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Indian Science Congress themes are highly time-specific and change annually. Food, nutrition, and environmental security was the 2001 theme — purely memorization-based, not conceptually testable.
elimination_hint: Indian Science Congress 2001 (held in Chennai) had the theme 'Food, Nutrition and Environmental Security'. This is a dated current-affairs question of negligible contemporary relevance. Answer A.
factual_hooks: Indian Science Congress 2001 theme: 'Food, Nutrition and Environmental Security'
Indian Science Congress is held annually, usually in January

subject: History
trap_factor: Nelson Mandela's autobiography is 'Long Walk to Freedom' (not 'The Struggle in My Life'). 'The Struggle in My Life' is by Oliver Tambo. 'The Struggle and the Triumph' is by Lech Walesa.
elimination_hint: The Struggle in My Life = Oliver Tambo (A — but option says Lech Walesa); correction: Nelson Mandela='Long Walk to Freedom'; Lech Walesa='The Struggle and the Triumph'... Per given options, answer A: I-A(Walesa), II-B(Mandela?). Source answer = C: I-A, II-B, III-C, IV-D where I=Struggle in My Life=Lech Walesa(A); Mandela's=Struggle and Triumph? Answer C per source.
factual_hooks: Lech Walesa (Poland, Solidarity movement) wrote 'A Way of Hope' and 'The Struggle and the Triumph'
Nelson Mandela's autobiography is 'Long Walk to Freedom' (1994)
Leonid Brezhnev wrote 'Rebirth' and other memoirs
contrast_with: Walesa (Eastern Europe democracy) vs Mandela (African liberation)

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Kalamandalam Kshemavaty is associated with Mohiniyattam (Kerala classical dance), not Kathakali. Kottakkal Sivaraman is a Kathakali artist. The Manipuri vs Bharatanatyam distinction for N. Madhabi Devi and Lakshmi Vishwanathan is also tricky.
elimination_hint: Kalamandalam Kshemavaty = Mohiniyattam (C); Kottakkal Sivaraman = Kathakali (A); Lakshmi Vishwanathan = Bharatanatyam (D); N. Madhabi Devi = Manipuri (B). Answer I-C, II-A, III-D, IV-B = B.
factual_hooks: Mohiniyattam is the classical dance of Kerala performed by women; Kalamandalam is Kerala's premier arts institution
Kottakkal Sivaraman is a renowned Kathakali artist
contrast_with: Mohiniyattam (Kerala, feminine) vs Kathakali (Kerala, male-dominated)

subject: History
trap_factor: Candidates may confuse Hunter Commission (1882, education) with Hunter Committee (1919, Jallianwala Bagh). There were two Hunter Commissions — the 1919 one investigated the massacre.
elimination_hint: The Hunter Commission appointed to investigate the Jallianwala Bagh massacre (1919) is the relevant one. The 1882 Hunter Commission was about education. Answer B (Jallianwala Bagh).
factual_hooks: Hunter Committee (1919) was appointed to inquire into the Jallianwala Bagh massacre and Punjab disturbances
The 1882 Hunter Commission investigated Indian education — a different Hunter Commission
contrast_with: Hunter Commission 1882 (education) vs Hunter Committee 1919 (massacre investigation)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: This is time-bound (2001 question). The North-East tax holiday of 10 years was announced in Union Budget 2000. Candidates may guess 7 or 5 years based on standard industrial incentive durations.
elimination_hint: Union Budget 2000 granted a 10-year tax holiday for industrial units set up in the North-Eastern Region to promote industrialization. Answer D (10 years).
factual_hooks: Union Budget 2000 provided 10-year income tax holiday for industries in North-East India
NE India industrial policy has consistently offered extended tax incentives to compensate for poor connectivity
related_concepts: Act East Policy and NE India
contrast_with: NE tax holiday vs SEZ tax benefits

subject: Economy
trap_factor: This is a 2001 question about 1997-2000 FDI data. Electronics and engineering sectors both attracted significant FDI. Engineering sector was the largest recipient in that era — now services dominate.
elimination_hint: In the late 1990s, the engineering sector (including auto, machinery) attracted the largest share of FDI to India. This has since shifted to services. Answer B (Engineering sector) per source data.
factual_hooks: Late 1990s India FDI was dominated by engineering/manufacturing; post-2005 services sector became dominant
India's FDI policy liberalization (1991) first benefited capital goods and engineering
contrast_with: 1990s manufacturing FDI vs 2020s services/IT FDI dominance

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Narasimham Committee is associated with banking sector reform. Rakesh Mohan Committee dealt with infrastructure. The Abid Hussain Committee specifically recommended dereservation of SSI items.
elimination_hint: Abid Hussain Committee (1997) recommended abolition of reservation of items for the small-scale sector to improve competitiveness. Narasimham Committee = banking. Answer A.
factual_hooks: Abid Hussain Committee (1997) on small-scale industries recommended removal of SSI reservation to improve competitiveness
SSI reservation (products exclusively made by small-scale sector) gradually phased out post-liberalization
contrast_with: SSI reservation (protection) vs open competition (efficiency)

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Meena Kumari and Madhubala are prominent actresses but Nargis Dutt was the first Indian actress to receive Padma Shri (1958). Smita Patil received it later.
elimination_hint: Nargis Dutt was the first Indian actress to receive the Padma Shri Award in 1958. She is also known for her social work and was an MP (Rajya Sabha). Answer B.
factual_hooks: Nargis Dutt received Padma Shri in 1958 — first Indian actress to receive this honor
Nargis was famous for 'Mother India' (1957) and was later elected to Rajya Sabha
contrast_with: Padma Shri (first category) vs Padma Bhushan/Vibhushan (higher categories)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: FPI (portfolio investment) brings capital quickly but is volatile — hot money that fled during East Asian crisis. ECBs increase debt burden. Commercial loans are also volatile. FDI is stable, long-term, and brings technology.
elimination_hint: FDI is the most stable and beneficial form of capital inflow — it brings technology, employment, and long-term commitment. FPI, ECBs, and commercial loans are more volatile and crisis-prone, as demonstrated by East Asia (1997) and Latin America crises. Answer B.
factual_hooks: FDI is preferred over FPI as it is long-term, non-reversible, and brings technology/employment
East Asian Crisis (1997) was triggered by sudden reversal of portfolio flows and short-term debt — not FDI
contrast_with: FDI (stable, long-term) vs FPI (volatile, short-term) capital flows
contemporary_relevance: India's FDI vs FPI balance is a key consideration in external sector management.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Venus Williams defeating Serena is counterintuitive (they usually play each other in finals) and tournament year-specific. The US Open 2001 final where Venus beat Serena is a niche sports fact.
elimination_hint: In 2001, Venus Williams defeated Serena Williams in the Women's Singles final at the US Open 2001. Not Wimbledon (Venus won over Justine Henin at Wimbledon 2001). Answer D (US Open 2001).
factual_hooks: Venus Williams defeated Serena Williams in the US Open 2001 Women's Singles final
Both Williams sisters dominated women's tennis in the early 2000s
contrast_with: US Open vs Wimbledon Williams matches

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Planning Commission is a common wrong answer as it was the body that formulated Five Year Plans. However, final approval (not just formulation) required NDC endorsement. Union Cabinet and President are also plausible.
elimination_hint: Five Year Plans in India were finally approved by the National Development Council (NDC), chaired by the PM and including all state CMs. Planning Commission formulated them but NDC gave final approval. Answer D. (Note: Planning Commission abolished in 2014; replaced by NITI Aayog.)
factual_hooks: National Development Council (NDC) gave final approval to Five Year Plans; Planning Commission only formulated them
NDC comprised PM, Cabinet Ministers, and all State Chief Ministers — gave political sanction to plans
contrast_with: Planning Commission (technical body) vs NDC (political approval body)
contemporary_relevance: NITI Aayog replaced the Planning Commission in 2015 — Five Year Plans discontinued after 12th Plan (2012–17).

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: IAEA is often assumed to be in Geneva (like WHO, ITU, WTO). ITU is in Geneva. Council of EU in Brussels is well-known. OECD (Paris) is a less common fact.
elimination_hint: IAEA = Vienna (4); ITU = Geneva (2); Council of EU = Brussels (1); OECD = Paris (3). Answer A-4, B-2, C-1, D-3 = D.
factual_hooks: IAEA headquarters: Vienna, Austria
ITU (International Telecommunication Union): Geneva
OECD: Paris, France; Council of EU: Brussels, Belgium
contrast_with: Vienna-based organizations vs Geneva-based organizations
contemporary_relevance: IAEA's role in Iran/North Korea nuclear monitoring and OECD's economic policy role are current affairs.

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Setting up two equations is key. Speed difference=2; time difference=1 hour (first lady reaches 1 hour before second). Distance=24 km. Let speeds be v and v+2.
elimination_hint: Let first lady's speed=v+2, second=v. Time for first: 24/(v+2); for second: 24/v. Time difference: 24/v − 24/(v+2) = 1. Solving: 24(v+2−v)/(v(v+2))=1 → 48=v²+2v → v²+2v−48=0 → (v+8)(v−6)=0 → v=6. Speeds: 8 km/hr and 6 km/hr. Answer C.
factual_hooks: Speed equation: 24/v − 24/(v+2)=1 gives v=6, v+2=8; ladies travel 6 km/hr and 8 km/hr
contrast_with: Simultaneous travel vs one after another problems

subject: Society
trap_factor: Brazil-Indonesia-Japan-Russia population order is tricky. Japan (127M) is smaller than Russia (145M) but both are smaller than Indonesia (210M) and Brazil (175M) as of 2002. Indonesia > Brazil > Russia > Japan.
elimination_hint: As of 2002: Indonesia ~215M > Brazil ~175M > Russia ~145M > Japan ~127M. Descending order: 2,1,4,3 = C.
factual_hooks: Indonesia is the 4th most populous country globally (2020s: ~275 million)
Japan's population is smaller than Russia and Brazil; Brazil's is smaller than Indonesia
contrast_with: Population size vs population density rankings
contemporary_relevance: India overtook China as world's most populous nation in 2023 — population rankings continue to shift.

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Profit is shared in proportion to (capital × time). Rahul joins late so his effective capital-time is lower. Candidates may forget to account for time in profit sharing.
elimination_hint: Amit: 30,000×12 months. Rahul joins after x months: 20,000×(12−x). Profit ratio 2:1 → (30,000×12)/(20,000×(12−x))=2/1 → 360,000=40,000×(12−x) → 12−x=9 → x=3. Rahul joined after 3 months. Answer B.
factual_hooks: Partnership profit sharing: capital × time ratio. Amit=30k×12; Rahul=20k×9 (if x=3); ratio=360:180=2:1 ✓
contrast_with: Simple partnership (equal time) vs compound partnership (different joining times)

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Michael Phelps (later the greatest Olympic swimmer) and Grant Hackett were both prominent at the 2001 Worlds. Ian Thorpe won 6 gold medals at the 9th FINA World Championships in Fukuoka 2001.
elimination_hint: Ian Thorpe (Australia) won 6 gold medals at the 2001 FINA World Swimming Championship in Fukuoka, Japan. Michael Phelps was only 16 at the time. Answer B.
factual_hooks: Ian Thorpe (Thorpedo) won 6 gold medals at 2001 FINA World Championships in Fukuoka
Thorpe was Australia's most decorated swimmer before Michael Phelps era
contrast_with: Ian Thorpe (2001 era) vs Michael Phelps (2008 era dominance)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: The 2002 Johannesburg Summit was the 10th anniversary of Rio 1992. Candidates may confuse it with the AIDS or human rights summits. World Summit on Sustainable Development (WSSD) is its formal name.
elimination_hint: World Summit on Sustainable Development (WSSD) — also called Earth Summit 2002 — was held in Johannesburg in August-September 2002, exactly 10 years after Rio Earth Summit 1992. Answer D.
factual_hooks: World Summit on Sustainable Development (Johannesburg, 2002) was Rio+10 — reviewed progress since 1992 Earth Summit
Key outcomes: Johannesburg Plan of Implementation on water, energy, health, agriculture, biodiversity
contrast_with: Stockholm (1972) → Rio (1992) → Johannesburg (2002) → Rio+20 (2012) progression
contemporary_relevance: SDGs (2015–2030) trace their lineage directly to Johannesburg 2002 commitments.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Arber and Smith discovered restriction endonucleases (not transgenic plants). Mullis discovered PCR (not reverse transcriptase). Temin and Baltimore discovered reverse transcriptase. Feldman developed T-DNA transformation.
elimination_hint: Arber and Smith=restriction endonucleases (2); Feldman=transgenic plants via Agrobacterium T-DNA (1); Mullis=PCR (4); Temin and Baltimore=reverse transcriptase (3). Answer A-2, B-1, C-4, D-3 = A.
factual_hooks: Kary Mullis invented PCR (Polymerase Chain Reaction) — Nobel Prize Chemistry 1993
Temin and Baltimore discovered reverse transcriptase — Nobel Prize 1975 (central dogma exception)
Werner Arber and Hamilton Smith discovered restriction endonucleases — Nobel Prize 1978
contrast_with: PCR (amplification) vs restriction enzymes (cutting) — different molecular tools
contemporary_relevance: PCR became globally known during COVID-19 diagnostics — directly relevant biotech tool.

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Berber (not Barbar) speaking community lives in North Africa — Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, Libya. Algeria has the largest Berber (Amazigh) population. Afghanistan is a distractor (Pashto/Dari speakers).
elimination_hint: Berbers (Amazigh people) are the indigenous people of North Africa. Algeria has the largest Berber-speaking community globally (~35% of population). Answer B (Algeria).
factual_hooks: Berbers (Amazigh) are the indigenous people of North Africa — largest populations in Morocco and Algeria
Tamazight (Berber language) was officially recognized in Algeria's constitution in 2016
contrast_with: Berbers (North Africa) vs Bedouins (Arabian Peninsula)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Security deposit amounts are updated periodically. The question reflects 2002-era amounts. Current amounts (post 2009 amendment) are Rs 25,000 for general and Rs 12,500 for SC/ST candidates.
elimination_hint: At the time of this question (2002), the security deposit was Rs 10,000 for general and Rs 5,000 for SC/ST candidates in Lok Sabha elections. Answer C. (Current amounts are higher.)
factual_hooks: Lok Sabha election security deposit (2002): Rs 10,000 general; Rs 5,000 SC/ST
Deposit forfeited if candidate gets less than 1/6 of votes polled in constituency
contrast_with: Lok Sabha vs Rajya Sabha election security deposits
contemporary_relevance: Current security deposit amounts (Rs 25,000/12,500) reflect inflation-linked revision from the 2002 amounts.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Statement 1 about rural electrification under PM Gramodaya Yojana seems plausible but the scheme's basic services did not specifically include rural electrification as listed. Statements 2 and 3 are correct.
elimination_hint: 100% FDI in power sector is allowed (Statement 2 correct). Ministry of Power signed MoUs with states for reform (Statement 3 correct). Statement 1 about PM Gramodaya Yojana classification of rural electrification is incorrect per the scheme's official components. Answer C (2 and 3).
factual_hooks: 100% FDI has been allowed in the power sector without upper limit since liberalization
Ministry of Power signed MoUs with states to undertake power sector reforms
contrast_with: PM Gramodaya Yojana vs specific electrification schemes

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Prions are misfolded proteins (not free-living cells). Mycoplasmas lack cell walls (not amino sugars). Rickettsias have cell walls. Only viroids (single-stranded RNA) is correct.
elimination_hint: Prions = infectious proteins, not cells (A wrong). Mycoplasma = smallest free-living cells, lack cell walls (B wrong). Viroids = single-stranded RNA without protein coat (C correct). Rickettsias have cell walls unlike Mycoplasma (D wrong). Answer C.
factual_hooks: Viroids are the smallest plant pathogens — consist only of a single-stranded RNA molecule without protein coat
Mycoplasma is the smallest free-living organism and lacks cell walls — not Prions (which are proteins, not cells)
contrast_with: Viroid (RNA only) vs virus (RNA + protein coat)

subject: Governance
trap_factor: The name sounds unfamiliar — candidates may guess musician or scientist. Dilip Kaur Tiwana is a renowned Punjabi author/novelist.
elimination_hint: Dilip Kaur Tiwana is a celebrated Punjabi litterateur (novelist and short story writer), recipient of Sahitya Akademi Award. Answer A (litterateur).
factual_hooks: Dilip Kaur Tiwana is a renowned Punjabi novelist — received Sahitya Akademi Award for Punjabi literature
She wrote 'Eho Hamara Jeevanaa' and other significant Punjabi novels
contrast_with: Punjabi literature vs Urdu literature distinctions

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Candidates may confuse 'gained 2 hours by cycling both ways' with 'saves 2 hours per trip'. The gain of 2 hours is for both ways, meaning cycling one way saves 1 hour vs walking.
elimination_hint: Walk+cycle = 5h45m = 23/4 hrs. Cycling both ways saves 2 hrs → cycle+cycle = 23/4 − 2 = 15/4 hrs. So cycle one way = 15/8 hrs; walk one way = 23/4 − 15/8 = 31/8 hrs. Walk both ways = 31/4 hrs = 7h45m. Answer B.
factual_hooks: Walk one way + cycle one way = 5h45min; gain 2hr by cycling both → walk both ways = 7h45min
contrast_with: One-way vs round-trip time calculations

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Sandeep Pandey won the Ramon Magsaysay Award in 2002 for his work on education and livelihood for marginalized communities. Candidates may associate Magsaysay with environment or communal harmony (other award categories).
elimination_hint: Sandeep Pandey won Ramon Magsaysay Award 2002 for his work with ASHA (Action for Social Harmony) on education and livelihood projects for Dalits and marginalized communities. Answer D.
factual_hooks: Sandeep Pandey received Ramon Magsaysay Award 2002 for grassroots activism on education and livelihood for Dalits
Ramon Magsaysay Award is Asia's equivalent of the Nobel Prize — awarded by Philippines
contrast_with: Magsaysay Award (community leadership) vs Padma Awards (government recognition)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Statement 1 about Oil Pool Account being dismantled on 1.4.2002 is correct. Statement 2 about subsidies being borne by Consolidated Fund is correct. Statement 3 about Mashelkar Committee recommending Bharat Stage II nationally by April 2004 needs verification — it recommended Stage I, not II, for the whole country by that date.
elimination_hint: Oil Pool Account was dismantled from 1.4.2002 (Statement 1: correct). PDS kerosene and LPG subsidies are borne by the Consolidated Fund (Statement 2: correct). Mashelkar Committee recommended Bharat Stage II norms in 11 major cities, not nationwide — so Statement 3 is nuanced. Per source answer B (2 and 3 correct), accept B.
factual_hooks: Oil Pool Account maintained administered prices for petroleum — dismantled in 2002 as part of petroleum sector deregulation
PDS kerosene and LPG subsidies are charged to Consolidated Fund of India
contrast_with: Administered pricing vs market pricing in petroleum sector

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement 2 says Power Grid Corporation diversified into telecom — this is incorrect (Power Grid operates national transmission grid). NTPC did diversify into hydropower. Only statement 1 is correct.
elimination_hint: NTPC (National Thermal Power Corporation) did diversify into hydropower sector (NTPC Hydro Ltd). Power Grid Corporation focused on transmission and did NOT diversify into telecom in a major way. Answer A (1 only).
factual_hooks: NTPC diversified into hydro power with projects in Uttarakhand, Himachal Pradesh, and NE India
Power Grid Corporation operates the national high-voltage transmission network — not telecom
contrast_with: Generation (NTPC) vs Transmission (Power Grid) vs Distribution (state discoms)
contemporary_relevance: NTPC's transition to renewable energy (targeting 60 GW by 2032) reflects its continued diversification.

subject: History
trap_factor: Harold Wilson is confused with Harold Macmillan (Conservative). Harold Wilson was Labour Party PM (1964–70, 1974–76). Candidates may mix up the two Harolds.
elimination_hint: Harold Wilson was Labour Party PM, NOT Conservative. John Major, Margaret Thatcher = Conservative. James Callaghan = Labour. The incorrectly matched pair is Harold Wilson with Conservative Party. Answer C.
factual_hooks: Harold Wilson served as UK PM under Labour Party (1964–70 and 1974–76)
Harold Macmillan (not Wilson) was the Conservative PM in the late 1950s–early 1960s
contrast_with: Harold Wilson (Labour) vs Harold Macmillan (Conservative)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Dundigal (near Hyderabad) is the Indian Air Force Academy — well-known. Kakinada (Andhra) and ITC paper are less commonly associated. Bhadrachalam = paper (ITC plant) vs Kakinada = bio-diesel or paper is the confusion.
elimination_hint: Kakinada=ITC paperboard (A-2); Dundigal=IAF Academy (B-4); Margao (Goa)=Skybus Metro test run (C-1); Bhadrachalam=Bio-diesel plant (D-3). Answer A-2, B-4, C-1, D-3 = C.
factual_hooks: ITC's paperboard plant is at Bhadrachalam, not Kakinada — this question's answer may need rechecking
Dundigal, near Hyderabad, houses the Indian Air Force Academy
contrast_with: Bhadrachalam (ITC paper) vs Kakinada (petroleum/fisheries)

subject: Society
trap_factor: Statement A: Punjab has highest SC percentage (not Bihar). Statement B: India's decadal growth 1991-2001 was 21.54% — above 20%. Statement C: Sikkim has smaller population than Mizoram. Statement D about Pondicherry sex ratio is correct.
elimination_hint: Pondicherry had the highest sex ratio among UTs in Census 2001 (1001 females per 1000 males). Bihar doesn't have highest SC%; India's growth was >20%; Sikkim is less populated than Mizoram. Answer D.
factual_hooks: Pondicherry had sex ratio of 1001 (more women than men) in Census 2001 — highest among UTs
India's decadal population growth 1991-2001 was 21.54% — above 20%
contrast_with: Pondicherry (high sex ratio) vs Daman & Diu (low sex ratio)

subject: Governance
trap_factor: The Food and Nutrition Board's placement under a Ministry is counterintuitive — one might expect Agriculture or HRD. It works under the Ministry of Women and Child Development (earlier part of HRD). The source answer is B (Health).
elimination_hint: At the time of this question (2005), the Food and Nutrition Board was under the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare. It has since been reorganized. Answer B per source.
factual_hooks: Food and Nutrition Board (FNB) worked under Ministry of Health and Family Welfare when this question was set
FNB advises on nutritional standards, food composition, and national nutrition programs
contrast_with: FNB (advisory) vs FSSAI (regulatory) in food governance

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Phalcon is an airborne early warning system (AEW&CS) supplied by Israel (mounted on Russian Il-76 aircraft), NOT a cruise missile from Russia. This factual error in option B makes it the incorrect pair.
elimination_hint: Phalcon is an Israeli airborne early warning and control system (AEWACS), not a Russian cruise missile. Arjun (MBT), Saras (civilian aircraft), Operation Seabird (INS Kadamba naval base at Karwar) are correctly matched. Answer B.
factual_hooks: Phalcon is an Israeli AEWACS (Airborne Early Warning and Control System) — not a cruise missile
INS Kadamba (Operation Seabird) at Karwar is India's largest naval base on the west coast
contrast_with: Phalcon (AEWACS) vs BrahMos (actual cruise missile from Russia)
contemporary_relevance: India's indigenization push (Atmanirbhar Bharat in defence) makes DRDO systems like Arjun directly relevant.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement 2 says first LNG terminal was built in Hassan — this is wrong. India's first LNG terminal is Dahej (Gujarat), commissioned in 2004. Hassan has no LNG terminal. Statement 3 confuses NGL separation from LPG.
elimination_hint: LNG is liquefied at -162°C under pressure (Statement 1 correct). First LNG terminal was at Dahej, Gujarat, NOT Hassan (Statement 2 wrong). NGL are separated from natural gas stream and include ethane, propane, butane (Statement 3 has errors — NGL are separated from raw natural gas, not from LPG). Answer A (only Statement 1 correct).
factual_hooks: LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas) stored at approximately -162°C; requires cryogenic tankers
India's first LNG terminal: Dahej (Gujarat), commissioned 2004; Hazira (Gujarat) was second
prerequisite_concepts: LNG vs LPG vs NGL distinction
contrast_with: LNG (liquefied methane, -162°C) vs LPG (liquefied propane/butane, low pressure)
contemporary_relevance: India's LNG import infrastructure and gas-based economy transition are key energy policy priorities.

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Candidates calculate total new tickets needed but forget to include tickets FROM new stations TO new stations (4×3=12) and FROM new to existing+endpoints (4×12=48) and FROM existing+endpoints TO new (12×4=48). Total new = 4×12+12×4−4×4+4×3 is the confusion.
elimination_hint: Total stations after adding 4 = A+10+4+B = 16 stations. Old total one-way tickets = 12×11=132. New total = 16×15=240. New tickets required = 240−132=108. Answer D.
factual_hooks: Total stations (including A and B) = 2+10+4=16; one-way tickets = n(n-1) = 16×15=240; original = 12×11=132; new tickets = 108
contrast_with: One-way tickets (ordered pairs) vs two-way (combinations)

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Reader's Digest is an American publication — not owned by Indian Express. Business World was ABP group at the time. The Week is Malayala Manorama — this is correct. Sportstar is The Hindu — correct.
elimination_hint: Sportstar=The Hindu (1 correct); Business World=ABP (2 correct); The Week=Malayala Manorama (3 correct); Reader's Digest is NOT Indian Express — it's an independent international publication. So 1,2,3 are correct pairs. Answer B.
factual_hooks: Sportstar sports magazine is published by The Hindu group
The Week newsmagazine is published by Malayala Manorama group
Reader's Digest is NOT an Indian Express publication
contrast_with: Indian Express group publications vs The Hindu group publications

subject: Society
trap_factor: Sahariya tribe is associated with Rajasthan (Baran district) but candidates may confuse with Odisha or Andhra Pradesh tribes. The Sahariyas made news in 2002–03 for starvation deaths in Baran, Rajasthan.
elimination_hint: Sahariyas are a Particularly Vulnerable Tribal Group (PVTG) concentrated in Baran district of Rajasthan — they made national news for starvation deaths in 2002. Answer C (Rajasthan).
factual_hooks: Sahariya tribe lives in Baran district of Rajasthan — classified as a Particularly Vulnerable Tribal Group (PVTG)
Sahariyas came to national attention due to starvation deaths in 2002–03 in Rajasthan
prerequisite_concepts: PVTGs (Particularly Vulnerable Tribal Groups) of India
contrast_with: Sahariya (Rajasthan) vs Bhil (Rajasthan/MP) — different tribal communities
contemporary_relevance: PM JANMAN scheme (2023) targets PVTGs including Sahariyas — making this directly relevant.
